Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Focus: Affordable, nutritious, sustainable, and reliable food access for students
Representative Stakeholders: Students; Vendors; Suppliers; Delivery actors; University; Operator; Regulator
Core Stakeholder Missions: Students obtain meals; vendors produce meals; suppliers provide ingredients; delivery actors transport meals; university safeguards welfare; regulator ensures compliance
Key Resources: Food ingredients; cooking capacity; student budget; vendor labor; delivery capacity; campus facilities; policy budget
Expected Outputs: Served meals; reduced waste; affordable menus; nutrition improvement; vendor viability; student satisfaction
Value Tokens / Value Signals: Money; subsidy; satisfaction score; vendor profit; EVI; trust; regulatory compliance
Dominant PSKVE Exchanges: Product: meals and ingredients; Service: preparation and delivery; Knowledge: demand forecasts; Value: payments and subsidies; Environment: waste and emissions
RMOV Accounts to Track: Student affordability and satisfaction; vendor capacity and profit; supplier stock and reliability; university welfare budget; ecosystem EVI
PUDAL Control Focus: Forecast demand, understand bottlenecks, recommend production and routing, execute stakeholder actions, learn from waste and satisfaction outcomes
MSRS Recommendation Targets: Menu choice; vendor routing; production quantity; supplier timing; delivery allocation; subsidy level; compliance inspection
Digital Twin Mini-Lab Adaptation: Reference implementation for Baseline, Online Platform, and MOEE comparison
Scenario Examples: Exam week; inflation; supply disruption; vendor failure; plastic ban; multi-shock crisis
Evaluation Metrics: Fulfillment; EVI; waste reduction; nutrition score; affordability; satisfaction; vendor profit; resilience
Transferability of MOEE Components: High for PSKVE, RMOV, MIG, PUDAL, and MSRS; domain-specific for menus, nutrition, and waste coefficients
Domain-Specific Constraints: Food safety; hygiene; perishability; class schedules; price sensitivity; campus policy
Research Contribution: Demonstrates how MOEE improves multi-stakeholder service coordination under operational and sustainability constraints

Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Focus: Support elderly dignity, narrative identity, cognitive-emotional well-being, and social connectedness
Representative Stakeholders: Elderly persons; Family; Caregivers; Church/community volunteers; Health professionals; AI story agents; Platform operator
Core Stakeholder Missions: Elderly share life stories and maintain meaning; family stays connected; caregivers detect risk; community provides companionship; platform coordinates support
Key Resources: Life stories; memory cues; family time; caregiver attention; health signals; emotional support; digital devices
Expected Outputs: Narrative records; well-being insights; social connection; stimulation recommendations; early risk signals; living autobiography
Value Tokens / Value Signals: GRACE index; attention; trust; companionship; story completeness; emotional stability; caregiver response
Dominant PSKVE Exchanges: Product: story artifacts; Service: companionship and care; Knowledge: life-history insights; Value: emotional well-being and trust; Environment: home/community context
RMOV Accounts to Track: Elderly emotional resource; mission to narrate and connect; story output; well-being value; caregiver time and response capacity
PUDAL Control Focus: Perceive story entries and mood signals, understand narrative identity, decide stimulation, act through prompts or alerts, learn from response patterns
MSRS Recommendation Targets: Story prompts; reminiscence questions; family engagement; caregiver attention; social stimulus; risk escalation
Digital Twin Mini-Lab Adaptation: Multi-agent elderly-care simulation with story agents, family agents, caregiver agents, and environmental stressors
Scenario Examples: Loneliness increase; memory decline; family absence; caregiver overload; festival season; health event
Evaluation Metrics: GRACE index; story frequency; emotional tone; connection frequency; response latency; risk detection accuracy
Transferability of MOEE Components: High for mission intensity, PUDAL, stakeholder utility, and MSRS; specialized for narrative and ethical care signals
Domain-Specific Constraints: Privacy; informed consent; vulnerability; non-coercion; emotional safety; clinical boundaries
Research Contribution: Extends MOEE from transactional coordination to human-centered care and narrative-value ecosystems

Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Focus: Provide reliable, affordable, and sustainable water access for households
Representative Stakeholders: Households; Water source operators; Pump/reservoir operators; Delivery actors; Warung air; Village authority; Maintenance team
Core Stakeholder Missions: Households obtain water; operators move and store water; authority maintains fairness; maintenance team ensures continuity
Key Resources: Water source; pump energy; reservoir capacity; pipes; carts or delivery vehicles; money; maintenance labor
Expected Outputs: Delivered water; stable reservoir level; affordable price; reduced downtime; maintenance response
Value Tokens / Value Signals: Water credits; money; service reliability; queue time; household satisfaction; maintenance status
Dominant PSKVE Exchanges: Product: water; Service: pumping, transport, maintenance; Knowledge: demand and level data; Value: payment; Environment: watershed and infrastructure condition
RMOV Accounts to Track: Household water stock; operator energy and capacity; reservoir level; maintenance resource; village service value
PUDAL Control Focus: Monitor source and demand, understand shortage risk, decide pumping and delivery schedule, execute flows, learn seasonal demand
MSRS Recommendation Targets: Pump schedule; reservoir release; delivery route; household allocation; price/subsidy; maintenance priority
Digital Twin Mini-Lab Adaptation: Water-flow and token-flow simulation with source, reservoir, delivery, and household agents
Scenario Examples: Dry season; pump failure; price shock; contamination event; demand surge; road disruption
Evaluation Metrics: Water fulfillment; outage frequency; affordability; reservoir stability; maintenance delay; fairness; resilience
Transferability of MOEE Components: Very high; PSKVE/RMOV naturally map to water and payment flows
Domain-Specific Constraints: Hydraulic capacity; public health; water rights; seasonal variation; infrastructure failure
Research Contribution: Shows MOEE as a coordination framework for physical-resource distribution and value-token exchange

Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Focus: Coordinate lender-borrower matching that improves repayment, trust, and value co-creation
Representative Stakeholders: Borrowers; Lenders; Loan arranger; Guarantors; Regulators; Credit-data providers; Community organizations
Core Stakeholder Missions: Borrowers obtain useful capital; lenders receive fair return; arranger reduces mismatch; regulator protects fairness and risk
Key Resources: Loan capital; borrower capability; credit history; collateral; trust; repayment capacity; risk information
Expected Outputs: Loan matches; repayment plans; risk scores; borrower value creation; lender return; reduced default
Value Tokens / Value Signals: Money; interest; risk score; trust score; repayment record; social value; regulatory compliance
Dominant PSKVE Exchanges: Product: loan capital; Service: matching and monitoring; Knowledge: risk and mission profile; Value: repayment and interest; Environment: market and regulation
RMOV Accounts to Track: Borrower resource and mission output; lender capital and return; arranger coordination value; regulator compliance
PUDAL Control Focus: Perceive borrower-lender states, understand risk and mission fit, decide loan structure, act through contract, learn from repayment outcomes
MSRS Recommendation Targets: Lender-borrower match; loan amount; repayment schedule; interest range; guarantor need; intervention trigger
Digital Twin Mini-Lab Adaptation: Agent-based credit ecosystem with borrower missions, lender risk profiles, and repayment dynamics

Evaluation Metrics: Repayment rate; default risk; borrower mission achievement; lender return; fairness; trust; EVI
Transferability of MOEE Components: High for RMOV, stakeholder utility, PUDAL, and MSRS; PSKVE needs financial-risk specialization

Value Tokens / Value Signals: Donation; volunteer hours; attendance; care response; trust; spiritual and social value
Dominant PSKVE Exchanges: Product: materials and facilities; Service: worship, care, programs; Knowledge: needs and schedules; Value: donations and trust; Environment: community context
RMOV Accounts to Track: Volunteer capacity; donation fund; program mission; pastoral-care output; community value
PUDAL Control Focus: Perceive needs, understand urgency, decide service allocation, act through teams, learn from participation and impact

Dominant PSKVE Exchanges: Product: relief goods; Service: rescue, transport, health care; Knowledge: needs maps; Value: aid funding and trust; Environment: disaster conditions
RMOV Accounts to Track: Household needs and relief received; agency stock; volunteer capacity; logistics routes; welfare value
PUDAL Control Focus: Perceive needs and hazards, understand bottlenecks, decide allocation, act through dispatch, learn from field feedback

Application Domain: Local Food Security and Healthy Food Supply

Representative Stakeholders: Households; Farmers; Food processors; Vendors; Health promoters; Local government; Delivery actors
Core Stakeholder Missions: Households access healthy food; farmers sell produce; vendors serve meals; government improves public health
Key Resources: Agricultural produce; processing capacity; market stalls; nutrition knowledge; transport; subsidy funds
Expected Outputs: Healthy food availability; farmer income; affordable meals; reduced malnutrition risk; reduced waste
Value Tokens / Value Signals: Money; subsidy; nutrition score; health indicator; farmer income; household satisfaction
Dominant PSKVE Exchanges: Product: produce and meals; Service: processing and distribution; Knowledge: nutrition advice and demand forecasts; Value: payments and health value; Environment: land and waste
RMOV Accounts to Track: Household nutrition resource; farmer output and income; vendor menu output; government public-health value
PUDAL Control Focus: Understand demand, supply, and nutrition gaps; recommend supply, menu, subsidy, and education actions
MSRS Recommendation Targets: Crop allocation; vendor menu; household purchase; subsidy targeting; nutrition campaign; delivery timing
Digital Twin Mini-Lab Adaptation: Community food-security model extending campus food to wider geographic and public-health context
Scenario Examples: Harvest failure; price inflation; nutrition campaign; transport disruption; subsidy expansion; seasonal demand

Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Focus: Reusable abstraction for any ecosystem where stakeholders exchange resources, outputs, knowledge, and value to fulfill missions
Representative Stakeholders: Users; Sources; Operators; Regulators; Intermediaries; Support actors; Environment
Core Stakeholder Missions: Each stakeholder transforms resources into outputs that create value for itself and others
Key Resources: Domain-specific resources; time; capacity; knowledge; infrastructure; trust; money or tokens
Expected Outputs: Mission outputs; exchanged products/services; improved stakeholder value; ecosystem resilience
Value Tokens / Value Signals: Money; credits; reputation; satisfaction; impact score; EVI; compliance signal
Dominant PSKVE Exchanges: Product, Service, Knowledge, Value, and Environment flows among stakeholder accounts
RMOV Accounts to Track: Resources, Mission, Output, and Value accounts for each stakeholder
PUDAL Control Focus: Perceive states, understand dependencies, decide recommendations, act through stakeholder missions, learn from outcomes
MSRS Recommendation Targets: Stakeholder actions; resource allocation; timing; routing; policy; incentives; compliance
Digital Twin Mini-Lab Adaptation: Domain template where stakeholders, RMOV accounts, exchanges, scenarios, policies, and metrics are configured
Scenario Examples: Demand shock; supply shock; cost shock; capacity loss; regulation change; behavioral shift; compound disruption
Evaluation Metrics: EVI; mission achievement; stakeholder utility; sustainability; resilience; fairness; robustness; acceptance rate
Transferability of MOEE Components: Highest; serves as the reusable meta-model for cross-domain implementation
Domain-Specific Constraints: Must be supplied by the target domain; no universal constraint set
Research Contribution: Positions MOEE as a general systems-engineering framework for mission-oriented multi-stakeholder ecosystems
